title: Wedelia oblonga
intro: Wedelia oblonga, also known as the Mexican daisy or false daisy, is a flowering plant species in the sunflower family (Asteraceae) that is native to Mexico and Central America. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Wedelia oblonga include flavonoids, terpenoids, and saponins, which have been shown to exhibit significant pharmacological activities, including antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial effects. In traditional medicine, Wedelia oblonga is commonly prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is used to treat a range of conditions, including fever, arthritis, and skin infections.
taxonomy: Wedelia oblonga is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This flowering plant is native to tropical regions and belongs to the genus Wedelia, which comprises various species of flowering plants. The genus Wedelia is further classified under the subfamily Helianthoideae and tribe Heliantheae. The classification of Wedelia oblonga reflects its evolutionary relationships and shared characteristics with other plants within the Asteraceae family. By understanding its classification, researchers can better appreciate the plant's adaptations and ecological roles in its native habitats.

It is believed to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which contribute to its medicinal uses. The plant is also used to treat fever, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. Its leaves and flowers are used in the treatment of digestive problems, such as diarrhea and dysentery. Additionally, Wedelia oblonga is used as an antiseptic to treat wounds and promote healing.

side_effects: Wedelia oblonga is not a well-documented plant, but Wedelia oblonga is commonly known as a medicinal plant in some parts of the world, and it may have similar side effects to other Wedelia species.
